original_reasoning: Arabic damagha = strike on the brain, smash the skull. Greek diatmego = cut apart, cleave, separate. Brain-striking and cutting-apart share the violent splitting core. Strong concrete link via cleaving action.
'Move forward / drive on' can plausibly drift to forceful outward pouring or abundance.
One Arabic sense is an eloquent tongue, and λέσχη includes talk or gossip, giving a plausible speech-centered semantic chain.

Arabic khamash = scratch, claw, leave sharp small marks; khumash = remnants of vengeance. Greek aichmas = acc.pl of aichme 'spear-point, sharp tip'. The 'sharp scratching/piercing' core matches directly. Strong concrete link.
Arabic nullified or displaced argument aligns with Greek asunder through breakup or disintegration.
Arabic slipping from the hand or choking stands on the opposite pole of Greek 'have, hold', preserving the same possession-loss field.
Bridehood strongly associates with adornment, luxury, and rich color, so sea-purple fits as a plausible specialization of bridal finery.
Arabic points to orderly structuring; Greek means 'interwoven with gold'. Both meet in the idea of arranged interweaving.
